title: Jet Fuel Crisis: 'No Shortage' vs. 84% Price Surge and 13,000 Canceled Flights
narrative: UK Department for Transport and Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander say 'no need' to change travel plans, no current jet fuel shortage. Airlines UK says 'no flights are being cancelled due to fuel shortages.' Industry groups advise the impact is 'marginal.' Abta says holidays should 'go ahead as planned.' Official message: the situation is under control, supply is stable, only cost has increased.
telemetry: Jet fuel prices up 84% since February 2026 (EASA data: from $831/tonne to ~$1,500/tonne)
IEA warning: Europe faces jet fuel shortages by June if supply isn't secured
Airlines have cut 13,000 flights globally in May (1% of global flights, per Cirium data)
Munich and Istanbul worst-hit destinations; Lufthansa removing 20,000 flights from summer schedule
EU formally authorized importing US jet fuel — an implicit admission domestic supply cannot meet demand
EASA guidance explicitly warns of potential fuel rationing during peak summer season
UK imports 65% of jet fuel, heavily dependent on Middle Eastern supply routes now disrupted
analysis: The official 'no shortage' message is technically true in a narrow sense — tanks aren't dry today. But it obscures a managed scarcity that regulators are already planning for. The IEA warning of shortages by June is not a separate story from the current 'no shortage' message — it's the direct consequence of the conditions that message is supposed to be addressing. When the UK's own agency projects rationing in 4-6 weeks and the transport secretary says 'no need to change plans,' one of those statements is designed to manage the news cycle rather than describe reality. The 13,000 canceled flights and 84% price surge are not a marginal impact. They are the signal. The reassurance is the noise.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Officials say no shortage. IEA projects rationing by June. 13,000 flights already canceled. Something doesn't add up.
